title: Applied Pine Board Fireplace Mantel
summary: A standalone shelf mantel built entirely from stock pine boards and applied moulding — a small applied piece, not the floor-to-ceiling framed surround built elsewhere in this catalog.
description:
This mantel is a self-contained applied shelf, not a framed structure built around an actual firebox — it's sized and built to mount to a wall on its own, giving a room a mantel shelf even where no fireplace exists. Lay out the top board and legs first, joining them on the back side with pocket holes if you have a jig, or glue and finish nails if not.

Stacking several 1x3 front decorative pieces is what gives the face its layered, built-up look rather than a single flat board — **glue these on rather than nailing**, since nails are likely to split this thin trim stock. A 1x8 board and short 1x3 blocking pieces added to the back bring the mantel out to its full depth, and this is also where you'll set the reveal: lay the crown moulding against the front assembly to check where the 1x4 trim piece lands, aiming for about a 2-1/2" reveal below the crown once it's mitered on.

Crown moulding wraps the top with mitered outside corners, and an optional inset square of edge moulding on the front face — glued in place — adds a final decorative detail if you want it.

tools.note: Optional; glue and finish nails work without one.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut the boards
steps.body: Cut the top board 48" from the 1x12 and the two legs 32 3/4" from the 1x10. From the 1x3 cut four front decorative pieces 44", the lower front trim 33 1/4", and two back blocking pieces 7 3/4"; cut the two side trim pieces 44" from the 1x6, the upper front trim 33 1/4" from the 1x4, and the back depth board 51" from the 1x8. The three 8 ft 1x3s are exactly consumed by their parts, with one board packing out to a full 96" — square one factory end of each and work from it rather than assuming both ends are true, because there is no slack to recover a bad cut. Leave the crown and the optional edge moulding long until the mantel is assembled and you can measure the actual corners.

steps.title: Lay out the top and legs
steps.body: Lay the 48" top board face down and stand a 32 3/4" leg under each end, square to the top and set in far enough that the trim will cover the joint. Fasten from the back side only — pocket holes into the top edge of each leg if you have a jig, otherwise glue plus finish nails driven where trim will hide them — so the visible top face stays free of fasteners. Measure from each end of the top to its leg and make the two readings match, then check the assembly sits flat on the bench before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add depth at the back
steps.body: Fasten the 51" back depth board and the two 7 3/4" back blocking pieces behind the legs and top; these are what bring the mantel out from the wall to its full depth. The back board runs 51" against the 48" top, so it projects 1 1/2" past each end — set that overhang deliberately and equally, since the back board is what registers against the wall when the mantel is hung. Glue and screw or nail from the back, and keep the fasteners clear of where you will later drive the wall screws.

steps.title: Set the front trim reveal
steps.body: Hold the crown moulding against the top of the front assembly to see where its bottom edge will land, then position the 33 1/4" upper front trim so about 2 1/2" of it stays visible below the crown once it is mitered on. Mark that reveal line right across the face before fastening anything, and set the 33 1/4" lower front trim from the same line. The whole layered face is built off this one line, so a mark placed by eye here moves every piece above and below it.

steps.title: Glue on the decorative front pieces
steps.body: Glue the four 44" 1x3 decorative pieces to the front face, stacked edge to edge, and clamp or weight them until the glue grabs. Glue only, no nails: 3/4" x 2 1/2" trim splits readily this close to its edges, and a face-to-face glue joint is stronger than any brad you could put through it. At 44" these pieces leave 2" of the 48" top overhanging at each end — measure that at both ends before the glue sets, because once these four boards are down the front cannot be shifted.

steps.title: Attach the side trim
steps.body: Nail the two 44" 1x6 side trim boards on, keeping their outside edges flush with the pieces they meet. They share the 44" length of the decorative front pieces, so dry-fit them against the assembly and mark their position rather than assuming they return around the ends — the plan's numbers do not settle that. Use 1 1/4" brads and glue, setting the nail heads below the surface so filler will hide them, and check that the front and side pieces meet without a step, since the crown returns land on that corner and will telegraph any gap.

steps.title: Add the crown moulding
steps.body: Miter the crown's outside corners with the moulding held against the saw fence at its actual spring angle, not laid flat — crown cut flat leaves a gap at the corner that no amount of filler will hide. Cut a test corner in an offcut first and check it closes. Watch the stock as you go: the 48" front plus two returns the depth of the 11 1/4" top comes to about 70 1/2" of a 72" stick, so there is barely an inch and a half of slack before miter waste and one bad cut costs you the piece. Glue every joint and pin with 1 1/4" brads.

steps.title: Add the inset moulding (optional)
steps.body: If you want the inset detail, lay the edge moulding out as a square on the front face, centred within the decorative band, and miter all four corners. Cut all four pieces before gluing any of them, so you can adjust the square as a set rather than chasing an error around it. Glue it on rather than nailing — the moulding is thin and the surface behind it is already trim over trim, so a brad has nothing solid to bite.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill every nail hole, sand through the grits, and pay particular attention to the built-up front, where the seams between the decorative pieces are the detail people actually look at. Vacuum and wipe the dust off, then prime and paint, or stain and seal. Secure the finished mantel to the wall by screwing through the back depth board into studs — a piece this size is stable standing but should never be left free to tip.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Applied pine board fireplace mantel shelf
